levers.name: MitID Ecosystem Refactoring
levers.consequences: Refactoring the MitID ecosystem to decouple from Apple/Google platform integrity signals enables a more modular and standards-based trust model. However, this approach may require significant upfront investment in re-certifying and re-profiling assets.
options: Decouple MitID's authentication logic from platform-controlled signals
Incorporate open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn for cross-platform authentication
Implement a modular trust architecture with plug-and-play components
levers.review_lever: Refactoring the MitID ecosystem to decouple from Apple/Google platform integrity signals creates a tension between preserving existing investments in asset re-profiling and certification, versus embracing open standards and modular architectures that may offer better long-term flexibility. However, this approach misses the complexity of establishing an entirely new fallback authentication path with its own unique set of requirements and risks.

levers.name: AltID Procurement Influence
levers.consequences: Influencing AltID procurement to include platform-neutral or fallback-capable access paths requires effective engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and related public-sector stakeholders. However, this approach may face resistance from existing operators and institutional inertia.
options: Secure inclusion of platform-neutrality language in official AltID design documents
Influence procurement requirements to mandate fallback-capable access paths for critical public authentication
Establish a coalition with civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee
levers.review_lever: Influencing AltID procurement to include platform-neutral or fallback-capable access paths creates a tension between securing formal written responses from relevant authorities and navigating the complexities of institutional inertia and incumbent resistance. However, this approach misses the need for credible technical demonstrations to underpin procurement language and standards pressure.

levers.name: Demonstrator Development
levers.consequences: Developing non-production demonstrators showcasing platform-neutral or fallback-capable access models requires a lean scope, defensible timelines, and credible technical evidence. However, this approach may not directly address procurement language, standards pressure, or institutional engagement.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator authenticating against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment
Explore a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path reducing dependence on the mobile OS duopoly
Produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google
levers.review_lever: Developing non-production demonstrators showcasing platform-neutral or fallback-capable access models creates a tension between producing credible technical evidence and influencing procurement language, standards pressure, and institutional engagement. However, this approach misses the importance of reframing the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, and supplier concentration risk.

levers.name: EU-Level Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework requires a strong coalition, credible technical evidence, and effective advocacy. However, this approach may face challenges in securing formal traction and influencing broader European requirements.
options: Secure inclusion of platform-neutral access language in European Digital Identity Wallet materials
Influence EU-level procurement requirements to mandate fallback-capable access paths for critical public authentication
Establish a coalition with civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, and sympathetic members of the European Parliament
levers.review_lever: Engaging with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework creates a tension between securing formal traction and navigating the complexities of institutional inertia, incumbent resistance, and financing uncertainty. However, this approach misses the importance of reframing the issue in terms of supplier concentration risk and continuity of service.

levers.name: Coalition Building
levers.consequences: Building a coalition spanning civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee requires effective engagement, credibility, and advocacy. However, this approach may face challenges in securing formal written responses from relevant authorities.
options: Establish a coalition with civil-society organizations and privacy advocates to influence AltID procurement
Build a coalition with security experts and contingency-planning stakeholders to address supplier concentration risk and continuity of service
Influence EU-level engagement by establishing a coalition with sympathetic members of the European Parliament
levers.review_lever: Building a coalition spanning civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee creates a tension between securing formal written responses from relevant authorities and navigating the complexities of institutional inertia and incumbent resistance. However, this approach misses the importance of credible technical demonstrations to underpin procurement language and standards pressure.

levers.name: Regulatory Framing
levers.consequences: Reframing the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, supplier concentration risk, and reduced strategic autonomy requires effective engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, and related public-sector stakeholders. However, this approach may face resistance from existing operators and institutional inertia.
options: Secure inclusion of platform-neutrality language in official AltID design documents
Influence procurement requirements to mandate fallback-capable access paths for critical public authentication
Establish a coalition with civil-society organizations, privacy advocates, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee
levers.review_lever: Reframing the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, supplier concentration risk, and reduced strategic autonomy creates a tension between securing formal written responses from relevant authorities and navigating the complexities of institutional inertia and incumbent resistance. However, this approach misses the importance of credible technical demonstrations to underpin procurement language and standards pressure.

levers.name: MitID Ecosystem Refactoring
levers.consequences: Refactoring the MitID ecosystem involves rewriting significant portions of the existing codebase to accommodate a platform-neutral fallback authentication path, which will incur substantial development costs. This might also lead to compatibility issues with older versions of the system.
options: Deep-embed FIDO2/WebAuthn directly into the MitID client as a primary authentication method, bypassing Apple/Google's platform integrity APIs.

Implement a hybrid approach where the MitID client uses both FIDO2/WebAuthn and platform-controlled signals for authentication, creating a complex trust model that requires careful configuration and ongoing maintenance.

Migrate the entire MitID system to an open-source framework like OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable Android-compatible platform, providing a clean slate but also introducing significant technical debt.
levers.review_lever: The options miss addressing the issue of vendor lock-in in the development tools and frameworks used for refactoring the MitID ecosystem. The project should consider the long-term implications of choosing specific development tools and frameworks that might be controlled by foreign vendors.

levers.name: AltID Procurement Influence
levers.consequences: Influencing AltID procurement decisions to include platform-neutral requirements and certified fallback authentication paths may lead to short-term delays in the rollout of the eIDAS2 wallet, but will ultimately ensure that Denmark's digital identity infrastructure is more resilient and less dependent on foreign technology suppliers.
options: Develop a strong coalition with civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee to push for platform-neutral requirements in AltID procurement.

Engage directly with the AltID procurement process, providing technical evidence and standards pressure to make supplier-neutral design harder to exclude, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability concerns.
Propose a separate RFP for the development of a certified fallback authentication path that can be used by Danish digital identity systems, allowing for more flexibility in the long term.
levers.review_lever: The options miss considering the potential impact on existing suppliers and their relationships with AltID stakeholders. The project should explore ways to mitigate any negative effects on these relationships.

levers.name: Demonstrator Development
levers.consequences: Developing non-production technical demonstrators will provide credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. However, this might also lead to increased costs and resource requirements.
options: Create a mobile-oriented demonstrator authenticating against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS.
Develop a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether, providing an alternative to existing systems.
Build multiple demonstrators exploring different scenarios and use cases, allowing for more comprehensive testing and validation.
levers.review_lever: The options miss addressing the issue of resource allocation and prioritization in demonstrator development. The project should carefully consider how to allocate resources effectively and prioritize the most critical demonstrators.

levers.name: EU-Level Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework will help align Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. However, this might also lead to increased complexity and coordination efforts.
options: Develop a strong coalition with other EU member states advocating for platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms in the European Digital Identity Wallet framework.
Engage directly with EU bodies and technical working groups to influence the development of standards and requirements that support platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms.
Propose amendments to existing EU regulations and directives to include provisions for platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms.
levers.review_lever: The options miss considering the potential impact on existing EU frameworks and regulations. The project should explore ways to ensure that any proposed changes are aligned with existing policies and do not create unnecessary complexity.

levers.name: Coalition Building
levers.consequences: Building a strong coalition of civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee will provide the necessary support for pushing platform-neutral requirements in AltID procurement. However, this might also lead to increased coordination efforts.
options: Develop a strong coalition with civil-society organisations and privacy advocates to push for platform-neutral requirements in AltID procurement.
Engage directly with security experts and contingency-planning stakeholders to provide technical evidence and standards pressure for supplier-neutral design.
Propose the creation of a new working group or task force to bring together relevant stakeholders and coordinate efforts.
levers.review_lever: The options miss addressing the issue of coalition sustainability and long-term commitment. The project should consider how to maintain momentum and ensure ongoing support from coalition members.

levers.name: Regulatory Framing
levers.consequences: Reframing the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk will help address privacy and democratic accountability concerns. However, this might also lead to increased regulatory complexity.
options: Develop a strong narrative around the importance of platform-neutral requirements for Danish digital identity infrastructure.
Engage directly with relevant regulatory bodies to influence the development of new regulations and standards that support platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms.
Propose amendments to existing regulations and directives to include provisions for platform-neutral access and certified fallback mechanisms.
levers.review_lever: The options miss considering the potential impact on existing regulatory frameworks. The project should explore ways to ensure that any proposed changes are aligned with existing policies and do not create unnecessary complexity.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Access Requirements
levers.consequences: Developing platform-neutral access requirements for Danish digital identity infrastructure will provide a clear direction for future development and procurement decisions. However, this might also lead to increased costs and resource requirements.
options: Develop detailed technical specifications for platform-neutral access requirements.
Create a set of guidelines and best practices for implementing platform-neutral access in Danish digital identity systems.
Propose the creation of a new standard or certification program for platform-neutral access.
levers.review_lever: The options miss addressing the issue of resource allocation and prioritization in developing platform-neutral access requirements. The project should carefully consider how to allocate resources effectively and prioritize the most critical requirements.

levers.name: Standards Harmonization
levers.consequences: Conforming to open standards like FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or comparable platforms facilitates a platform-neutral fallback path, but standardization bodies may resist altering existing trust models that benefit incumbent vendors.
options: Forge an alliance with relevant EU bodies to revise the eIDAS and NSIS frameworks
Propose amendments to Denmark's digital identity standards to prioritize open-platform compatibility
Leverage industry-wide standardization efforts, such as the FIDO Alliance, to promote platform-neutral authentication protocols
levers.review_lever: The project assumes that influencing procurement decisions and shaping standards will suffice to establish a platform-neutral fallback path without directly confronting existing trust models or vendor interests. However, this overlooks the potential for vendors to adapt and lobby against changes in their favor.

levers.name: Digital Sovereignty Campaigning
levers.consequences: Public awareness campaigns can build momentum behind digital sovereignty goals, but may struggle to overcome entrenched interests or be co-opted by politicians seeking to capitalize on the issue for their own agendas.
options: Launch a nationwide public education campaign highlighting Denmark's digital identity vulnerabilities and the benefits of platform-neutral access
Partner with influential media outlets and thought leaders to amplify the project's message and build credibility
Organize town hall meetings, panel discussions, and other community events to engage citizens in the digital sovereignty debate
levers.review_lever: The campaign may inadvertently create a polarized public discourse that alienates potential allies or emboldens opponents, rather than fostering constructive dialogue around digital sovereignty.

levers.name: Technical Showcase
levers.consequences: Demonstrators showcasing platform-neutral authentication capabilities can provide concrete evidence of feasibility and stimulate interest among stakeholders, but may be perceived as a 'proof-of-concept' rather than a fully-fledged solution.
options: Develop a mobile-oriented demonstrator that leverages FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or comparable platforms
Design a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly
Create a hybrid demonstrator combining multiple authentication methods to showcase flexibility and adaptability
levers.review_lever: The technical focus may lead stakeholders to prioritize short-term gains over long-term strategic benefits, such as overlooking potential security risks associated with adopting new technologies.

levers.name: Regulatory Advocacy
levers.consequences: Influencing regulatory bodies can shape Denmark's digital identity landscape, but may require navigating complex bureaucratic processes and building coalitions with diverse stakeholders.
options: Engage with relevant EU institutions to revise the eIDAS and NSIS frameworks in favor of platform-neutral access
Advocate for amendments to Denmark's digital identity standards to prioritize open-platform compatibility
Partner with industry associations and lobby groups to promote a unified message on digital sovereignty
levers.review_lever: The project may struggle to balance competing interests among regulatory bodies, industry stakeholders, or civil society organizations, potentially diluting its impact or creating unintended consequences.

levers.name: AltID Integration
levers.consequences: Integrating platform-neutral access requirements into AltID can ensure a seamless transition to a more resilient digital identity infrastructure, but may require significant changes to existing systems and processes.
options: Develop a phased integration plan that gradually introduces platform-neutral access capabilities within AltID
Propose a wholesale overhaul of AltID's architecture to prioritize open-platform compatibility and fallback authentication paths
Leverage industry-wide standardization efforts, such as the FIDO Alliance, to promote platform-neutral authentication protocols within AltID
levers.review_lever: The integration may be delayed or resisted by stakeholders who prefer maintaining the status quo or are hesitant to invest in new technologies and processes.

levers.name: EU-Level Engagement
levers.consequences: Engaging with EU bodies can help align Denmark's digital identity efforts with broader European goals on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in, but may require navigating complex institutional dynamics.
options: Partner with relevant EU institutions to revise the eIDAS and NSIS frameworks in favor of platform-neutral access
Advocate for amendments to Denmark's digital identity standards to prioritize open-platform compatibility
Leverage industry-wide standardization efforts, such as the FIDO Alliance, to promote platform-neutral authentication protocols within the EU
levers.review_lever: The project may struggle to influence EU-level policies and regulations, potentially limiting its impact on Denmark's digital identity landscape.

levers.name: Procurement Influence
levers.consequences: Influencing procurement decisions can shape the development of AltID and related digital identity systems, but may require navigating complex bureaucratic processes and building coalitions with diverse stakeholders.
options: Engage with relevant EU institutions to revise the eIDAS and NSIS frameworks in favor of platform-neutral access
Advocate for amendments to Denmark's digital identity standards to prioritize open-platform compatibility
Partner with industry associations and lobby groups to promote a unified message on digital sovereignty
levers.review_lever: The project may struggle to balance competing interests among regulatory bodies, industry stakeholders, or civil society organizations, potentially diluting its impact or creating unintended consequences.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season adds a fixed cost that erases the per-unit savings unless utilization reaches year-round levels."
     - "Each additional clinical site requires its own IRB approval, site-initiation visit, and staff credentialing — a sequential overhead of 8–14 weeks per site that compounds rather than parallelizes, so doubling site count does not halve enrollment time."
     - "Migrating the rendering pipeline to GPU compute cuts frame time from 12 ms to 4 ms in benchmarks, yet every asset authored for the CPU path must be re-profiled, and the team has no GPU debugging toolchain — the migration cost is hidden in artist-hours, not engineering-hours."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")
   - NO starting review_lever with "The options", "These options", "The lever", or "These levers" as the grammatical subject — name a specific mechanism, constraint, or risk instead

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.
